questions.question: Explain the difference between framebuffer objects (FBOs) and renderbuffer objects (RBOs) in OpenGL. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: FBO is a container that holds attachments (textures/renderbuffers)
RBO is a data storage buffer optimized for rendering
Textures can be sampled in shaders, renderbuffers cannot
Prefer RBOs for depth/stencil (faster)
Prefer textures for post-processing and readable results

questions.question: What is double buffering in OpenGL? Explain how it eliminates screen tearing with a diagram description.
keyPoints: Uses front and back buffers
Back buffer is drawn to while front buffer is displayed
Buffers are swapped after rendering completes
Swapping is synchronized with display refresh
Prevents partial frames from being visible (tearing)

questions.question: List and explain the three main types of attachments possible in a framebuffer object.
keyPoints: COLOR attachment - stores pixel color values (RGBA)
DEPTH attachment - stores depth/Z values for depth testing
STENCIL attachment - stores integer values for stencil testing/masking
Each serves different purpose in rendering pipeline
Attachments can be textures or renderbuffers

questions.question: What is framebuffer completeness check? Why is it important? List the conditions for a complete framebuffer.
keyPoints: glCheckFramebufferStatus checks if FBO is properly configured
Returns GL_FRAMEBUFFER_COMPLETE if valid
Conditions: color attachment has image, all attachments complete, same sample count, proper dimensions
Prevents undefined behavior during rendering
Should be called after setting up FBO before use

questions.question: Explain the concept of off-screen rendering using FBOs. Give two practical applications.
keyPoints: Rendering to FBO instead of default framebuffer
Used for: 1) Post-processing effects (bloom, blur)
2) Shadow mapping - rendering from light's perspective
3) Reflections - render scene to texture
4) Dynamic textures - real-time generated content
Output stored in texture for further shader processing

questions.question: What is the swap chain in OpenGL? Explain how V-sync affects framebuffer swapping.
keyPoints: Swap chain manages sequence of buffers presented to display
Buffers are swapped (front↔back) after rendering completes
V-sync synchronizes swap with monitor refresh rate
Without V-sync: screen tearing (partial frames visible)
With V-sync: smooth but may limit frame rate to monitor Hz
Modern: adaptive V-sync balances both

questions.question: Write pseudocode to create and use an FBO with color and depth attachments for off-screen rendering.
keyPoints: 1. Generate FBO: glGenFramebuffers(1, &fbo)
2. Bind FBO: glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, fbo)
3. Create and attach color texture: glGenTextures, glTexImage2D, glFramebufferTexture2D(GL_COLOR_ATTACHMENT0)
4. Create and attach depth RBO: glGenRenderbuffers, glRenderbufferStorage, glFramebufferRenderbuffer(GL_DEPTH_ATTACHMENT)
5. Check completeness: glCheckFramebufferStatus
6. Use: glBindFramebuffer to render, then glBindFramebuffer(0) to display

questions.question: Describe the complete process of setting up a Framebuffer Object (FBO) in OpenGL with both color and depth attachments. Include function calls, parameters, and the completeness check.
keyPoints: Step 1: Generate FBO name using glGenFramebuffers
Step 2: Bind FBO using glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, fbo)
Step 3: Create texture for color attachment - glGenTextures, glBindTexture, glTexImage2D with format (RGBA)
Step 4: Attach color texture - glFramebufferTexture2D(GL_COLOR_ATTACHMENT0, GL_TEXTURE_2D, colorTex, 0)
Step 5: Create renderbuffer for depth - glGenRenderbuffers, glBindRenderbuffer, glRenderbufferStorage(GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT)
Step 6: Attach depth RBO - glFramebufferRenderbuffer(GL_DEPTH_ATTACHMENT)
Step 7: Check completeness - glCheckFramebufferStatus, compare with GL_FRAMEBUFFER_COMPLETE
Step 8: Unbind FBO - glBindFramebuffer(GL_FRAMEBUFFER, 0)
Key considerations: matching dimensions, supported formats, sample counts

questions.question: Explain the rendering pipeline when using Double Buffering and the Swap Chain. How does this affect frame rate and visual quality? Discuss V-sync and its implications.
keyPoints: Pipeline: 1) Draw to back buffer (not visible)
2) Complete frame rendering
3) Swap buffers (back→front)
4) Display new front buffer
Swap Chain: manages buffer queue, enables continuous rendering
V-sync ON: swaps synchronized with monitor refresh (60Hz typical)
V-sync OFF: immediate swaps causing screen tearing
V-sync ADAPTIVE: enables if above refresh rate, disables if below
Triple buffering: 2 back buffers reduces latency
Trade-off: smoothness vs potential frame rate limitation

questions.question: Design and explain a post-processing pipeline in OpenGL using FBOs. Include multipass rendering concept, texture sampling, and full-screen quad rendering.
keyPoints: Pass 1: Render scene to FBO - bind FBO, render scene geometry to color texture
Pass 2: Post-process - bind default framebuffer, use FBO's color texture on full-screen quad
FBO Setup: Create texture for color attachment, optionally depth
Full-screen quad: Two triangles covering screen, UVs map to texture
Shader processing: Apply effects (blur, edge detection, color grading)
Blit: Use glBlitFramebuffer to resolve multisampled FBO if needed
Example effects: Bloom (extract bright→blur→combine), SSAO
Key functions: glDrawBuffers for MRT, glReadBuffer for sampling

questions.question: Compare single buffering, double buffering, and triple buffering. Illustrate with timing diagrams how each handles frame rendering and display synchronization.
keyPoints: Single Buffer: Draw directly to display buffer, visible tearing, no synchronization
Double Buffer: Draw to back, swap to front when done, eliminates tearing, may stall GPU
Triple Buffer: Two back buffers in queue, GPU always has work, lowest latency, smoother
Timing with V-sync: Frame time = 16.67ms (60Hz), if render > 16.67ms, frames dropped
Without V-sync: Immediate swap, tearing visible, higher FPS but poor quality
Triple buffer advantage: While front/back swap, third buffer being filled, no stall
Modern implementations: Adaptive sync (FreeSync/G-Sync) varies refresh rate dynamically
